text: The video shows a fluorescent colorectal anastomosis in a patient undergoing a high anterior resection for sigmoid cancer.
The surgeon observes the unhealthy appearance of the colon, indicating that the colorectal anastomosis needs to be redone.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic resection using a medial to lateral approach, executing a distal transection across the upper rectum.
The surgeon prepares the left mesocolon intercorporeally following the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, with visible manipulation of the mesocolon tissue.
The surgeon observes good perfusion in the marginal artery, which will be confirmed using indocyanine green (ICG) angiography.
The surgeon prepares to perform perfusion angiography using the Novadax endoscopic system, Pinpoint, to assess blood flow in the surgical field.
The view switches to near-infrared, revealing the inflow of indocyanine green (ICG) dye proximally to the designated point for proximal transection.
The near-infrared view displays a strong arterial inflow pattern, confirming brisk perfusion in the marginal artery following its transection.
The surgeon uses a stapler to perform the proximal transection, ensuring the cut is made within the area of good perfusion as indicated by fluorescence inflow.
The surgeon removes the specimen and prepares the proximal colon by placing a purse string suture and an anvil. The procedure then transitions to relaparoscopy.
The anvil in the proximal descending colon is visually aligned with the head of the circular stapler, which is inserted transanally into the rectum.
The surgeon switches to the near-infrared view to repeat the indocyanine green (ICG) angiography, visually assessing the perfusion of the anastomosis in situ.
The surgeon observes that the colon tissue surrounding the anvil exhibits slower fluorescence compared to adjacent tissues, indicating differences in perfusion.
The near infrared view shows the colon tissue around the anvil gradually brightening over thirty seconds, indicating delayed fluorescence compared to surrounding tissues.
The surgical field displays a black and white view in the middle left of the screen, highlighting specific anatomical structures or surgical instruments.
The surgeon fires the stapler at the anastomosis site, completing the stapling process in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon observes patchy fluorescence along the bowel, contrasting with the more uniform fluorescence of the proximal colon segment.
The video shows a segment of the colon with some arterial inflow, but it is less robust compared to the upstream section, as indicated by the fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon examines the mesocolon closely, identifying an area of encroachment at the same level as previously observed.
The video shows an injury to the mesocolon, where the marginal artery inflow appears preserved, but the venous outflow is visibly damaged.
The surgeon examines the bowel, observing visible signs of arterial perfusion in the tissue.
The white light image reveals a section of the colon with compromised appearance, indicating potential issues with perfusion or tissue health.
The video shows the intestinal segments where ICG has perfused out, highlighting a lack of perfusion in the targeted area, indicating potential perfusion issues.
The video shows the affected segment of the colon where indocyanine green (ICG) dye is visibly trapped due to venous congestion, highlighting the area of concern.
The surgeon performs a proximal transection across the descending colon, visibly cutting through the tissue.
The surgeon prepares the newly formed proximal colonic conduit, which will serve as part of the reconstructed intestinal pathway.
The surgeon performs a transection in the distal sigmoid colon, cutting through the tissue to separate it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgical field provides a clear view and easy access to the colorectal anastomosis, with surrounding tissues visible.
The surgeon performs further dissection of the mesorectum to ensure the distal transection is positioned below the previous staple line.
The surgeon freshens the rectal stump, removing any non-viable tissue to ensure the second anastomosis is implanted into healthy rectal tissue.
The surgeon redoes the anastomosis, connecting the newly formed proximal colonic conduit to the freshened rectal stump, ensuring the anastomosis implants into healthy rectal tissue.
The circular stapler head is visible as its spike emerges through the rectal stump, preparing for the anastomosis.

The surgeon manipulates the proximal colonic segment, identified as the very proximal descending colon near the splenic flexure. A suture is placed to repair a small serosal tear in the splenic flexure area, ensuring adequate length for the segment to reach the top of the rectal stump.
The surgeon performs an ICG angiogram, revealing the dynamic inflow pattern along the colon, indicating proper blood supply.
